race.name: RAGBRAI
race.display_name: RAGBRAI (Register's Annual Great Bicycle Ride Across Iowa)
race.tagline: America's oldest, largest, and longest multi-day recreational bicycle ride. 10,000+ riders. 7 days. ~400 miles across Iowa from the Missouri River to the Mississippi. Running since 1973. Where an entire state becomes your aid station.

vitals.location: Across Iowa, USA (Missouri River to Mississippi River, west to east)
vitals.date: Last full week of July annually (Saturday to Saturday)
vitals.date_specific: 2026: July 18-25 (RAGBRAI LIII). Route: Onawa to Dubuque.
vitals.field_size: ~10,000-20,000 registered weeklong riders + thousands of day riders and unregistered riders. Peak single-day attendance exceeded 50,000 (2023 RAGBRAI L, Ames to Des Moines).
vitals.registration: Online registration at ragbrai.com. Weeklong, non-rider, and day pass options available. Registration opens November each year.
vitals.entry_fee: $250 for weeklong rider (2026, before rate increase). $45 for weeklong non-rider. Day pass pricing varies.
vitals.feed_zones: Pass-through towns every 10-20 miles serve as de facto aid stations, with local communities providing food, water, entertainment, and bike repair. SAG (Support and Gear) wagons patrol the route for riders with medical or mechanical emergencies. Official RAGBRAI support includes main campground facilities, showers, bike shop, and info tent at each overnight town.
vitals.cutoff_time: No formal cutoff. Riders are encouraged to finish before dusk. SAG wagon support operates during published route hours only.
route_options: Weeklong: ~391-470 miles over 7 days (varies by year; 2026 is 391 miles, the shortest ever)
Day pass: ride one or more individual days
Century Loop: optional 100-mile extended route on select days
KARRAS Loop: shorter alternative loops available on some stages

climate.primary: Iowa late July — hot, humid, with potential severe thunderstorms
climate.description: Late July in Iowa is summer at its most intense. Daytime highs typically reach 85-95F (29-35C) with high humidity, creating heat indices well above 100F on the worst days. Mornings start in the 60s-70F with heavy dew. Afternoon thunderstorms are common and can be severe — Iowa sits squarely in the Great Plains storm track. Nighttime temperatures drop to the 50s-60s in camp. Wind is a factor, particularly on exposed farmland stretches, though the prevailing westerly tailwinds generally favor the west-to-east route. Riders will experience every Iowa summer weather condition across seven days.
challenges: Sustained heat and humidity — 85-95F with Midwest humidity making efforts feel brutal
Severe thunderstorms possible on any day, including lightning and high winds
Sun exposure across open Iowa farmland with minimal tree shade
Temperature swings from 55F overnight in camp to 95F+ afternoon riding
Wind exposure on open prairie — beneficial tailwinds most days but headwinds and crosswinds happen
Cumulative heat stress over 7 consecutive days of riding

terrain.primary: Paved Iowa roads — rolling hills, farm country, and river bluffs
terrain.surface: All paved roads. Iowa county roads and state highways through agricultural heartland. Western Iowa features the Loess Hills with steep, punchy climbs and fast descents. Central Iowa is gently rolling with long stretches through corn and soybean fields. Eastern Iowa gets hillier approaching the Mississippi River bluffs, with the final descent into river towns often being dramatic. Road surfaces vary from smooth state highways to rough chip-seal county roads.
features: Western Iowa Loess Hills: steep, punchy climbs through some of Iowa's hilliest terrain
Central Iowa farm country: long, rolling stretches through corn and soybean fields with wide-open views
Eastern Iowa bluffs: hilly terrain climbing toward the Mississippi River with dramatic descents into the final town
Pass-through towns every 10-20 miles: each one a mini-festival with food, music, and cheering crowds
Missouri River dip at the start and Mississippi River dip at the finish — the iconic RAGBRAI bookends
2026 route: 391 miles with 16,027 feet of climb — shortest but 10th hilliest in 53-year history

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 73. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 71.

logistics.lodging_strategy: Main campground access is included with registration — pack a tent, sleeping bag, and pad. Baggage trucks transport your gear daily. Charter services (Pork Belly Ventures, Pedaling Pandas, Padre's Cycle Inn) offer premium campgrounds with bunkhouses, shower trailers, meals, and concierge service ($1,500-2,500 for the week). Hotels, motels, and B&Bs in overnight towns exist but book up months in advance and vary wildly by town size. Host housing — generous Iowa residents opening their homes — can be requested after registration, with town housing forms typically available in March.

biased_opinion.summary:
RAGBRAI is not a race. It is not even really a ride, in the way most cyclists understand rides. It is a 400-mile rolling block party across the entire state of Iowa, powered by pedals and sustained by the most extraordinary host community in sports. You will ride through towns where the entire population has turned out to feed you pie, spray you with garden hoses, and blast classic rock from a flatbed trailer. You will camp in church yards, drink beer at breakfast, and discover that Iowa is not flat. You will dip your tire in the Missouri River on Saturday and the Mississippi River the following Saturday and wonder where the week went.

Since 1973, RAGBRAI has defined American recreational cycling. It inspired over 200 similar events worldwide. It has been visited upon all 99 Iowa counties. Nearly half a million riders have completed it. The oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride in the world is not a race — and that is precisely why it matters. RAGBRAI proves that the best reason to ride a bicycle is to ride a bicycle, preferably through a small town that has been waiting all year for you to arrive.

Do not come to RAGBRAI expecting a challenge. Come expecting a transformation. Come expecting the farmer playing saxophone on the hilltop at sunrise, the 90-year-old woman handing out cookies from her porch, and the moment on Day 5 when you realize you have been smiling for five straight days and you cannot remember the last time that happened. That is RAGBRAI. That is Iowa. That is cycling at its most fundamentally, unapologetically joyful.
strengths: The host communities: Nothing in cycling — or arguably all of sports — compares. Entire Iowa towns transform into festivals for riders. Residents open homes, yards, kitchens, and hearts.
The experience: Seven days of rolling cultural immersion. Pie stands, live music, slip-and-slides, church pancake breakfasts, pork chops on sticks, and 10,000 friends you have not met yet.
The value: $250 for a week of supported riding with baggage transport, campgrounds, SAG support, and a bike shop. Iowa is inexpensive. The cost-per-day-of-joy ratio is unmatched.
The legacy: 53 years. 474,000+ riders. All 99 counties. Over 200 imitator events worldwide. RAGBRAI is American cycling's foundational experience.
weaknesses: The heat: Late July Iowa is hot, humid, and prone to severe thunderstorms. The worst days feel brutal, and there is no mountain shade to hide in.
The crowds: 10,000+ riders on narrow county roads create bottlenecks, especially in small pass-through towns. Patience is required.
Not a challenge: If you need competitive racing, technical terrain, or serious climbing to feel fulfilled, RAGBRAI will not scratch that itch. The difficulty is cumulative, not acute.
Camping for a week: The default accommodation is a tent in a field. If sleeping on the ground for seven nights sounds miserable, budget for charter services or creative hotel arrangements.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: RAGBRAI is the event that every cyclist should do at least once. Not for the fitness, not for the challenge, not for the Strava data — but for the irreplaceable experience of riding across an entire state while an entire state cheers you on. There is nothing else like it.

final_verdict.one_liner: America's oldest, largest, and longest multi-day bicycle ride. 53 years of Iowa towns turning themselves inside out for 10,000+ riders.
final_verdict.should_you_race: You do not race RAGBRAI. You ride it. You experience it. You eat pie at every stop and drink beer before noon and sleep in a tent in a stranger's yard and discover that Iowa is hillier, friendlier, and more beautiful than you imagined. If you have ever wanted to know what happens when an entire state decides to throw a week-long party for cyclists, register for RAGBRAI. Registration opens November each year and fills steadily. $250 for the week. Bring a bike, a tent, sunscreen, and an appetite. Leave the power meter at home.
alternatives: Cycle Oregon (week-long supported ride across Oregon, ~500 miles, more climbing and scenery variety)
Bike Across Kansas (BAK) (8-day ride across Kansas, flatter, similar Midwest community spirit)
Pan-Mass Challenge (2-day Massachusetts charity ride, 177 miles, $1B+ raised for Dana-Farber)
AIDS/LifeCycle successor Cycle to Zero (3-day California ride, cause-driven, coastal)

videos.title: RAGBRAI 2023 - Our Crazy Bike Ride Across Iowa!
videos.description:
Bailey and I rode our first RAGBRAI this July and it was such a fun ride! RAGBRAI stands for the Registar's Annual Great Bike Ride Across Iowa and 2023 was the 50th anniversary. 

This year the ride was over 500 miles, had 28,000 registered riders, and HOT! Join us as we tackle our first RAGBRAI, meet some amazing people (@duzer ) and enjoy the Iowa countryside. 

Let me know if you have any questions on RAGBRAI. Happy to answer any in the comments below.

0:00 - Intro
0:19 - Day 1
3:18 - Day 2
7:06 - Day 3
10:41 - Day 4
12:56 - Day 5
15:55 - Day 6
18:25 - Day 7

My bike is the Trek Checkpoint ALR 5 and it was a great fit for RAGBRAI!

Learn more here: https://ragbrai.com

Originally from Nashville, Tennessee, we got married June 2013 and quickly caught the #travel bug! We started “travel hacking” & finally decided we wanted to go travel for one year. After 2 years of saving $ and over 2 million miles and points, we sold our cars and apartment and left home January 10, 2016. We started this #travelvlog to share our experiences with friends and family, then decided we really liked vlogging and traveling, and we didn’t want to stop after just one year! After achieving our new goal of 100 countries and then all 7 continents (check out our documentary here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FuFv4iF8wgg&t=1s), we had no plans to slow down, until 2020 decided to force us to ;) which is when we bought a converted Sprinter van to start exploring our own country for the first time. #Vanlife was a major adjustment and so different than flying every week like we were used to, but we are incredibly thankful to be able to continue doing something we love every day. 😊🎥🌎

videos.curation_reason: High-quality production documenting the complete RAGBRAI experience with unique tandem perspective and excellent storytelling.

videos.title: Nail Your First RAGBRAI: The Checklist That Covers It All!
videos.description:
Download the Ragbrai first-timers checklist here https://www.keepyourdaydream.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/RAGBRAI-First-Timers-Checklist.pdf

ℹ️ About the Episode: 
If you haven't heard of RAGBRAI, it stands for Register's Annual Great Bicycle Ride Across Iowa, and this year is the 50th anniversary. There were 30,000 bicyclists—a sight to see even if you don't ride—and the RV was the tool of choice for many riders, including us. It was up to Trish to navigate the truck and trailer solo across the state. This episode includes many special people, all up for the challenge of doing hard things. We look for opportunities that push us outside of our comfort zone and allow us to be a little more grateful for things we might take for granted. There's also the added benefit of knowing that if we can do this, we can do other things that are new and scary.
We don't normally recommend books we haven't read, but ​The Comfort Crisis: Embrace Discomfort to Reclaim Your Wild, Happy, Healthy Self​ comes highly recommended by Jeff. We know of the author, and we talked about this book during our 530-mile ride across Iowa. Truth be told, there isn't much we didn't talk about! You know we love books, and you might enjoy this one.
The other thing that made this challenge even more fun was the RAGBRAI checklist that Jeff & Deb created. You know how much we love lists, especially when it comes to making the most of an adventure. To get a copy of this list, click  ​here​.

❤️ KYD Insiders https://www.patreon.com/KeepYourDaydream 
Join the most supportive and genuine RV community ever. Get our welcome package in the mail, monthly ((Livestreams)), invitations to meet-ups, giveaways and chat in the private Insiders Facebook group. 

🍋 Tricia's Cookbook & Spice Blends http://tricialeach.com/
Cooking in a small space doesn't have to mean sacrificing nutrition or taste. Get Tricia's battle-tested tips, tricks, and recipes for preparing delicious, healthy meals in any kitchen.

🎬 YouTube Course https://howtostartayoutubechannel.com/ 
If you have ever thought about starting a YouTube channel or just want even better videos to document your journey, we share everything we've learned. Learn how to create, grow and monetize your channel without knowing anything about video or wasting time.

🎼MUSIC: 
Musicbed is our latest music source: https://www.musicbed.com
Artlist has been game-changing http://bit.ly/KYD-Artlist
Epidemic Sound: https://www.epidemicsound.com/

📸 SOCIAL MEDIA LINKS
http://www.Instagram.com/KeepYourDaydream 
http://www.Facebook.com/KeepYourDaydream

👨‍⚖️ Keep Your Daydream, All Rights Reserved
*This channel for entertainment purposes only. We are not RV experts and are not making recommendations. We share our opinions and what works for us, but you should do your own research and make your own decisions.

quotes.text: Over seven days we biked 515 miles from the Missouri to the Mississippi... day five was gonna be the hardest day on route, got 89 miles almost 4200 feet of elevation, it's gonna be a rough day, it's gonna be hot.

It's bike utopia and we all play a part in creating the magic. It's the best of humanity, it's medicine for my soul. Being here amongst all these extraordinary people is a great reminder as to why being human is such a wonderful thing.

Over the course of 7 days riders attempt to ride over 400 miles across the entire state. Each night they camp in a new town and the town throws a big party - there are thousands of people packed into this town.

terrain_notes.text: Riders pedal through beautiful cornfields and charming old towns across Iowa. The riding is very safe with pretty much zero traffic along the route.

gear_mentions.text: Riders used a tandem bicycle for the entire event. One rider mentioned picking up the tandem bike the afternoon before the event started.

race_day_tips.text: Lower your expectations and have fun. You need to pace yourself when eating because there are meetup towns spaced throughout the route.
Most riders pedal at conversation pace rather than racing speed. The police have a unique way of keeping everyone in line.
Get to first come first serve camping places early before crowds arrive to avoid having to set up in front of a crowd.

additional_quotes.text: You can pay $8 for a shower but she is currently shaving your legs with the fire Hydra
20,000 people put up tents and backyards city parks and school yards pretty much anywhere with green grass becomes a campground
RAGBRAI is the kind of event where you'll be walking down the street and get invited into a locals home for dinner and before you know it you're part of the family

rider_intel.search_text: RAGBRAI is a 7-day recreational bike touring event across Iowa covering 400-450 miles from Missouri River to Mississippi River. The route features beautiful cornfields and charming small towns with very safe riding conditions and practically zero traffic. Daily mileage ranges from 40-80 miles. Around 20,000 riders camp in host cities each night, setting up tents in backyards, city parks and school yards. The event showcases Iowa hospitality with locals opening their homes to riders. Food is abundant throughout the route including pork chops, pie, corn, and breakfast burritos. Most riders maintain conversation pace rather than racing speed. The riding is very social with entertainment every night. Riders traditionally start by dipping rear tire in Missouri River and end by dipping front tire in Mississippi River. Camping logistics require arriving early at first-come-first-serve locations to avoid crowds.
